# UC-19.1.3 · Firmware Compliance (Cisco UCS)

> **Criticality:** Medium &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Compliance, Inventory &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We keep a list of the software versions you approved for each part inside the servers. We compare that to what the machines report so you can plan upgrade waves in order and keep unsafe or odd mixes from taking you by surprise in production.*

---

## Description

Flags Cisco UCS B-series and C-series resources whose **running** firmware for out-of-band management, BIOS, adapters, and storage-class controllers no longer matches the customer’s written baseline, using periodic inventory from UCS Manager so drift shows up before compatibility testing gaps or unpatched security advisories do.

## Value

A single non-compliant line of firmware can void interoperability with a new hypervisor, leave a public advisory unpatched, or break a support matrix during an outage call. Catching baseline drift in one place—weeks before the maintenance window—lets infrastructure teams sequence bundle upgrades, align vendor RMAs, and document evidence for security governance without hand-auditing every blade.

## Implementation

Enable UCS Manager–polled `cisco:ucs:inventory` in `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, maintain `ucs_approved_firmware.csv` (component class → gold version), and schedule the compliance search for weekly reports and on-demand audits ahead of change freezes.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, Splunkbase 2731, v4.3.1 or newer) installed on the Heavy Forwarder or data-collection node that is allowed to open HTTPS to each UCS Manager VIP; the same add-on (or a lightweight TA-only copy) on Search Heads for lookups, knowledge objects, and saved-search ownership.
- Service account on UCSM with at least read-only access to the classes needed for inventory, including the `firmwareRunning` objects under server equipment DNs. Cisco documents role requirements in the add-on’s installation guide; do **not** reuse personal admin credentials in `inputs.conf`—use a dedicated `splunk_ucs_readonly` user whose password is rotated and stored in Splunk’s credential store where the input supports it.
- **Scope clarity:** this use case is written for **UCSM-managed** B-series (`sys/chassis-*/blade-*`) and C-series registered to UCSM (`sys/rack-unit-*`). If your only inventory path is Intersight for X-series compute, inventory fields differ—collect that stream with the Cisco Intersight add-on and treat it as a separate use case. Mention this in your data-governance pack so audit readers do not assume one search covers every Cisco rack.
- Network: the collection host must reach `https://<ucsm-vip>/` on 443/tcp (or your re-home port) without TLS interception that breaks the UCSM certificate chain. If a proxy is mandatory, add an exception; the add-on is not a browser and cannot complete interactive captive portals.
- Splunk role: the role running day-two searches must include `cisco_ucs` (or whatever index you chose in the input) in `srchIndexesAllowed`.
- License: inventory is comparatively low events-per-second versus per-second stats, but the poll frequency and domain size set volume—budget roughly one daily compliance report plus alert noise before tightening filters.

### Step 1 — Configure data collection and the baseline
(1) **Create or confirm the modular input** using either Splunk Web → **Settings → Data inputs** (look for the “Cisco UCS” family tied to the add-on) or by editing `local/inputs.conf` inside `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`. The shipped `default/inputs.conf` in the add-on (inspect it after install) documents the exact stanza pattern—commonly a modular input whose name is prefixed in the style `cisco_ucs://<descriptive_name>`; copy that pattern into `local/inputs.conf`, set `disabled = 0`, point `ucsm_url` to `https://<ucsm-fqdn>` (or the vendor-documented key name), and supply credentials. Reload the input if prompted.
(2) **Set the target index** (often `cisco_ucs` per Splunk and Cisco field guides; your organisation may use `os` or a security operations index). Keep `cisco:ucs:inventory` as the sourcetype the TA tags for inventory—do not override it in `props.conf` unless a competent Splunk admin owns the follow-on extraction fixes.
(3) **Enable inventory** in the add-on’s configuration UI/REST options so the poll includes MOs that back `firmwareRunning` entries. Polling period is typically 300–3600 seconds depending on your tolerance for staleness; firmware changes rarely, but security teams often want a daily refresh in highly regulated orgs. Document the number you chose in the CMDB so auditors understand latency.
(4) **Author the `ucs_approved_firmware` lookup** as a UTF-8 CSV in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/<your_custom_app>/lookups/ucs_approved_firmware.csv` (never edit under `default/` in the TA). Required columns: `component_type` (one of: `BIOS`, `Adapter`, `StorageController`, `CIMC`, `Other` to match the `case()` in the search), and `approved_version` (string exactly as UCSM reports it after you validate—see Step 3). Optional: `min_version` if you later extend SPL for semantic comparisons, and `notes` (free text, for example: Approved after CSCxxxx patch bundle 4.2(3a)). Define `transforms.conf` in the same app:
```
[ucs_approved_firmware]
filename = ucs_approved_firmware.csv
```
Reload authentication / bundle deploy if the search head cluster uses deployer shcluster-bundle.
(5) **Map organisational truth** to each row: export your internal “gold” firmware matrix (usually maintained by the compute platform team alongside Cisco’s release notes) into that CSV. If multiple UCS domains intentionally run different baselines, either split the lookup per domain using an extra `domain` column and extend the SPL, or run separate saved searches with `index`/`sourcetype` identical but a `where host=<HF>` filter if each Heavy Forwarder serves one domain.

### Step 2 — Create the search, report, and optional alert
Run this SPL after you have at least one day of `cisco:ucs:inventory` in the chosen index. If the first run returns no rows, remove the `classId` / `object_type` filters, run `| head 5 | table *`, and adjust field names in the `coalesce()` list to the ones your TA version really emits (Splunk field names are version-sensitive).

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-7d@d
| search (classId="firmwareRunning" OR object_type="firmware" OR object_type="firmwareRunning" OR (like(classId, "firmware%") AND isnotnull(dn)))
| eval running_version=trim(coalesce(version, running_version, running_fw, fw_version, ""))
| rex field=dn "^(?<server_mo>sys/(?:chassis-\d+/blade-\d+|rack-unit-\d+))"
| eval ldn=lower(dn)
| eval component_type=case(
    like(ldn, "%/bios%") OR like(ldn, "bios-%"), "BIOS",
    like(ldn, "%adaptor%") OR like(ldn, "%vic%"), "Adapter",
    like(ldn, "%sas%") OR like(ldn, "%storage%"), "StorageController",
    like(ldn, "%mgmt%") OR (like(ldn, "%board%") AND like(ldn, "%controller%")), "CIMC",
    1=1, "Other"
  )
| where isnotnull(server_mo) AND running_version!=""
| stats latest(running_version) as running_version, max(_time) as last_seen by server_mo, component_type
| lookup ucs_approved_firmware component_type OUTPUT approved_version, notes
| eval compliant=if(isnull(approved_version), "Unknown", if(running_version=approved_version, "Yes", "No"))
| eval days_since_seen=round((now()-last_seen)/86400, 1)
| sort compliant, component_type, server_mo
```

**Field rationale:**
– `firmwareRunning` is the standard UCS MO class for “what is live now” vs repository packages. The TA may mirror this into `classId` and/or a friendly `object_type`; the `search` clause keeps the query working across minor variations.
– `version` is the most common key for the running revision; other builds of the add-on or adapter-specific inventory rows sometimes alias it—hence the `coalesce` chain matching patterns already used in sibling UCs in this library (`running_fw` appears on adapter objects—validate which keys exist for the firmware class on your build).
– `server_mo` collapses the DN to the **server** anchor (`sys/chassis-…/blade-…` or `sys/rack-unit-…`) so compliance rolls up to a serviceable unit rather than to every subordinate `…/vnic-…` path.

**Alerting guidance (without wiring a specific product):** schedule a weekly saved search filtered to `compliant!="Yes"` with **suppress** for seven days on `server_mo` + `component_type` to avoid the same straggler every run. For regulated environments, add a second alert for `compliant="Unknown"` meaning the lookup row is missing (governance issue, not a host issue).

### Step 3 — Validate
(1) In Splunk, pick one **non-compliant** row. Copy its `server_mo` and `dn` (add `| table server_mo, dn, running_version, component_type` to the search temporarily).
(2) In UCS Manager GUI, open **Equipment** and navigate the tree to the same blade or rack (the left-hand path mirrors the left-hand side of the DN; `sys` maps to the root). Open **Inventory → Firmware** (or per-component tabs such as **BIOS**, **CIMC**, **Adapter**, **SAS** depending on UCSM 3.x/4.x UI) and locate the line whose distinguished name or component label matches the suffix of `dn` in Splunk. The **Running** version string must match `running_version` from Splunk; if the GUI label shortens a build number, that is a signal to change how you type `approved_version` or to store the canonical long form in the lookup.
(3) Cross-check another way: in UCSM launch the **KVM / CIMC** view only if you need OS-level context—firmware for this use case is authoritative from UCSM inventory, not from the guest OS `dmidecode` (guest OS can lag behind a pending power-cycle activation).
(4) For automation-first teams, you may optionally call `configResolveClass` for `firmwareRunning` in read-only form against the same UCSM API endpoint the add-on uses; the XML you receive should list the same `version` and `dn` as the Splunk event when timestamps align.
(5) Record screenshot evidence in your change system when you intentionally accept drift (for example, an emergency PSIRT) so the next Splunk result does not re-open a closed governance ticket.

### Step 4 — Operationalise
(1) Build a “Firmware posture” workspace with the visualisations from the `visualization` field: the heatmap answers “do we have surprise clusters?”, the bar chart answers “which class is dragging compliance?”, the table is the work queue for the server team.
(2) Tie `server_mo` to ownership using your CMDB or an optional second lookup that maps DNs to application owners—this is client-specific but prevents anonymous rack assets.
(3) Process link: when the eval result for compliant is No, open a change record referencing Cisco’s published bundle/infra guidance for the target release (see the Cisco doc link in `references`) and block production workload migration onto that host until the exception is closed.
(4) For reporting cadence, export the weekly saved search to CSV and e-mail the storage location your risk team already audits (restricted share, DLP-scanned mailbox, or ticketing attachment).
(5) Version-control the `ucs_approved_firmware.csv` in git with peer review, not silent spreadsheet edits, so you can show auditors who approved each baseline string and when.

### Step 5 — Troubleshooting

- **Zero `cisco:ucs:inventory` events** — the modular input is still disabled, credentials failed, or the Heavy Forwarder cannot route to UCSM. On the collection host, open `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/splunkd.log` and search for the add-on’s python modular input name; fix TLS trust first (import UCSM’s signing chain into the OS store if you terminate TLS on an internal CA). In Splunk, `index=_internal source=*splunkd.log* cisco*ucs*` often surfaces the exact REST error (401 vs 404 vs timeout).

- **Events exist, but nothing passes the `firmware` filter** — your TA build might tag firmware rows only with `classId=firmwareRunning` and leave `object_type` blank, or the inverse. Use a small probe search starting with `index=cisco_ucs` and `sourcetype=cisco:ucs:inventory` (unquoted in SPL), then add `| stats count by classId object_type`, to see the true pairs, then trim the `search` line in the main compliance SPL accordingly.

- **`running_version` always blank** — the key for version on `firmwareRunning` rows might be `version` vs `fwVer` (older adapters). Rerun a narrow time window, use `| head 1` and the Fields sidebar (or a short `| stats count` by candidate field names) to list the correct version key and extend the `coalesce` list. Never hard-code vendor guesses in production without one validation cycle (Step 3).

- **`server_mo` is null** — the DN pattern in your environment includes unusual forms such as `sys/ Rack-unit-` spacing or 9xxx rack identifiers not captured by the sample regex. Broaden the `rex` to include the shapes you see in a five-minute `| stats values(dn)` output.

- **All rows are `compliant` yet UCSM show a hotfix** — your `approved_version` may match a coincidental string while a **different** `firmwareRunning` row for a secondary controller still lags. Remove the `stats … by server_mo, component_type` temporary clause to list **all** DNs; you may be collapsing multiple incompatible rows into the latest single value. Consider grouping by `dn` in strict audits.

- **Frequent `Unknown` from missing lookup lines** — the CSV is missing a `component_type` you derive as `Other`. Add explicit rows, or change the `case()` mapping to align with the labels your org actually approves. Do not mark `Other` as “compliant by default” without formal risk acceptance.

- **X-series or Intersight-only servers in the same index** — you will get inventory-looking noise or partial rows that never match the UCSM baseline rules. Filter them out with `| where isnotnull(server_mo) AND !like(server_mo, "%x-%")` **only** if you have verified the naming in your DNs; better: send Intersight data to a different index and keep this report UCSM-pure.


## SPL

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-7d@d
| search (classId="firmwareRunning" OR object_type="firmware" OR object_type="firmwareRunning" OR (like(classId, "firmware%") AND isnotnull(dn)))
| eval running_version=trim(coalesce(version, running_version, running_fw, fw_version, ""))
| rex field=dn "^(?<server_mo>sys/(?:chassis-\d+/blade-\d+|rack-unit-\d+))"
| eval ldn=lower(dn)
| eval component_type=case(
    like(ldn, "%/bios%") OR like(ldn, "bios-%"), "BIOS",
    like(ldn, "%adaptor%") OR like(ldn, "%vic%"), "Adapter",
    like(ldn, "%sas%") OR like(ldn, "%storage%"), "StorageController",
    like(ldn, "%mgmt%") OR (like(ldn, "%board%") AND like(ldn, "%controller%")), "CIMC",
    1=1, "Other"
  )
| where isnotnull(server_mo) AND running_version!=""
| stats latest(running_version) as running_version, max(_time) as last_seen by server_mo, component_type
| lookup ucs_approved_firmware component_type OUTPUT approved_version, notes
| eval compliant=if(isnull(approved_version), "Unknown", if(running_version=approved_version, "Yes", "No"))
| eval days_since_seen=round((now()-last_seen)/86400, 1)
| sort compliant, component_type, server_mo
```

## Visualization

(1) Heatmap: `component_type` (rows) × `running_version` (columns) with cell colour by share of the fleet, highlighting unexpected version clusters. (2) Horizontal bar chart: count of distinct `server_mo` with `compliant="No"` by `component_type`. (3) Table: `server_mo`, `component_type`, `running_version`, `approved_version`, `compliant`, `last_seen` for drilldown to remediation tickets. (4) Single-value: percentage of in-scope `server_mo` where all rows are `compliant="Yes"` (treat `Unknown` per policy—either exclude or show as a second tile). (5) Optional timechart: count of new `No` rows per day to show progress during an upgrade wave.

## Known False Positives

Planned **rolling upgrade waves** where some blades already carry the new bundle and others are still on the last approved build until their window—accurate in reality, noisy if you expect one flat version everywhere. **Out-of-band hotfix** images applied for a one-off field-service action before the central baseline file catches up. **New rack or chassis** provisioned after the last quarterly lookup edit, so the row reads “No” until the spreadsheet owner updates `ucs_approved_firmware.csv`. **BIOS and management-controller trains** on different but jointly supported revs, while your lookup stores only a single string per `component_type` and therefore can’t represent paired rules without splitting rows. **Adapter firmware** whose GUI shows a marketing label but the API returns a longer build id string—comparisons look wrong until you normalise in the lookup’s `notes` and optional helper fields. **Capability-catalog auto-sync** in UCSM temporarily diverges from the human-maintained “gold” list during catalog imports.

## References

- [Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (Splunkbase 2731)](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731)
- [Cisco UCS Manager Firmware Management Guide, Release 4.2 (HTML)](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/unified_computing/ucs/ucs-manager/GUI-User-Guides/Firmware-Mgmt/4-2/b_UCSM_GUI_Firmware_Management_Guide_4-2/b_UCSM_GUI_Firmware_Management_Guide_chapter_010.html)
- [Integrate data with Splunk: Cisco UCS (Splunk documentation)](https://help.splunk.com/en/data-management/integrate-data-with-add-ons/splunk-supported-add-ons/splunk-supported-add-ons/cisco-ucs)
